chapters.title: Gravitation
notes: Gravitation: Gravitation is the force of attraction between any two objects having mass. It is a universal force that acts throughout the universe.
Newton's Observation: Sir Isaac Newton explained gravitation after observing a falling apple. He concluded that the Earth attracts every object towards its centre.

Universal Law of Gravitation: Every object attracts every other object with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
Universal Gravitational Constant (G): Represented by G. Its value is 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg² and remains constant throughout the universe.
Centripetal Force: The force acting towards the centre of a circular path is called centripetal force. Without this force, the object would move along the tangent to the circle.
Moon's Revolution: The Moon revolves around the Earth because Earth's gravitational force provides the necessary centripetal force. Planets revolve around the Sun for the same reason.
Kepler's First Law: Planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus of the ellipse.
Kepler's Second Law: A planet sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time. Therefore, planets move faster when closer to the Sun.
Kepler's Third Law: The square of the time period of revolution is proportional to the cube of the mean distance from the Sun.
Acceleration due to Gravity (g): Represented by g. On Earth's surface its average value is 9.8 m/s². It decreases with increasing height.
Weight: The gravitational force exerted by the Earth on an object. Weight changes from place to place because g changes.
Mass: The amount of matter present in an object. Mass remains constant everywhere in the universe.
Free Fall: Objects falling freely under gravity. During free fall, only gravitational force acts and velocity increases by 9.8 m/s every second.
Escape Velocity: The minimum speed required to leave Earth's gravitational field permanently. Its value for Earth is approximately 11.2 km/s.
Artificial Satellites: Man-made objects that revolve around the Earth due to gravitational force. Used for communication, weather forecasting and navigation.
Tides: The gravitational pull of the Moon causes tides in oceans. High and low tides occur due to the combined gravitational effects of the Moon and the Sun.

flashcards.q: What is gravitation?
flashcards.a: Gravitation is the force of attraction between any two objects having mass.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the Universal Law of Gravitation?
flashcards.a: Sir Isaac Newton.

flashcards.q: State Newton's Universal Law of Gravitation.
flashcards.a: Every object attracts every other object with a force directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

flashcards.q: What is the value of G?
flashcards.a: 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N m²/kg².

flashcards.q: What is centripetal force?
flashcards.a: The force acting towards the centre of a circular path.

flashcards.q: What is the value of acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
What is escape velocity for Earth?
flashcards.a: Approximately 11.2 km/s.

practice.q: State Kepler's three laws of planetary motion.
practice.a: First: Planets move in elliptical orbits with Sun at one focus. Second: Equal areas swept in equal time. Third: Square of time period ∝ cube of mean distance.

practice.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
practice.a: Mass is the amount of matter in an object and does not change with location. Weight is the gravitational force on an object and changes with location.

practice.q: Why does the Moon not fall towards the Earth despite gravitational attraction?
practice.a: The Moon's orbital velocity provides centripetal force that keeps it in orbit around the Earth.

practice.q: How does altitude affect the value of g?
practice.a: g decreases as altitude increases because gravitational force decreases with distance from Earth's centre.

practice.q: Name three uses of artificial satellites.
practice.a: Communication, weather forecasting and GPS navigation.

chapters.title: Periodic Classification of Elements
notes: Periodic Table: Elements are arranged systematically in the modern periodic table according to their atomic numbers. This arrangement helps in studying their properties easily.

Dobereiner's Law of Triads: Johann Dobereiner grouped elements in sets of three (triads) having similar properties. The atomic mass of the middle element was approximately the average of the other two.
Newlands' Law of Octaves: John Newlands observed that every eighth element showed similar properties to the first, when arranged in increasing atomic mass.
Mendeleev's Periodic Table: Dmitri Mendeleev arranged elements according to increasing atomic masses. He left gaps for undiscovered elements and accurately predicted their properties.
Limitations of Mendeleev's Table: Hydrogen and isotopes could not be placed properly. Atomic masses of some elements did not match their chemical properties.

Modern Periodic Law: Henry Moseley arranged elements according to increasing atomic number. The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
Structure of Modern Periodic Table: Has 7 periods (horizontal rows) and 18 groups (vertical columns). Elements in the same group have similar chemical properties.
Periods: A period is a horizontal row. Atomic number increases from left to right across a period.
Groups: A group is a vertical column. Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons.
Metals: Mainly present on the left side. They generally lose electrons to form positive ions. Metallic character decreases across a period and increases down a group.
Non-metals: Present on the right side. They usually gain electrons to form negative ions. Non-metallic character increases across a period and decreases down a group.
Metalloids: Possess properties of both metals and non-metals. Silicon and germanium are common examples.
Atomic Size: Decreases across a period because nuclear charge increases. Increases down a group as new electron shells are added.
Valency: Changes regularly across a period. Remains the same for elements in the same group.
Noble Gases: Placed in Group 18. They have completely filled outermost shells and are chemically inert.

flashcards.q: On what basis are elements arranged in the modern periodic table?
flashcards.a: Increasing atomic number.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the modern periodic law?
flashcards.a: Henry Moseley.

flashcards.q: How many periods and groups are in the modern periodic table?
flashcards.a: 7 periods and 18 groups.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the Law of Triads?
flashcards.a: Johann Dobereiner.

flashcards.q: Who proposed the Law of Octaves?
flashcards.a: John Newlands.

flashcards.q: Why are noble gases chemically inactive?
flashcards.a: Because they have completely filled outermost electron shells.

flashcards.q: What happens to atomic size across a period?
flashcards.a: It decreases from left to right because nuclear charge increases.

practice.q: What are the limitations of Mendeleev's periodic table?
practice.a: Hydrogen could not be placed properly. Isotopes of the same element had to be placed in different positions. Some elements with similar properties were placed in different groups.

practice.q: How does metallic character change across a period and down a group?
practice.a: Metallic character decreases across a period and increases down a group.

practice.q: Who prepared the first successful periodic table and how did he arrange elements?
practice.a: Dmitri Mendeleev prepared the first successful periodic table by arranging elements in order of increasing atomic masses.

practice.q: What is the difference between a period and a group in the periodic table?
practice.a: A period is a horizontal row where atomic number increases across it. A group is a vertical column where elements have the same number of valence electrons.

practice.q: Why do elements in the same group show similar properties?
practice.a: Because they have the same number of valence electrons and similar electronic configurations.

chapters.title: Chemical Reactions and Equations
notes: Chemical Reaction: A process in which one or more substances are converted into new substances with different properties. Involves breaking of old bonds and formation of new bonds.
Chemical Equation: Represents a chemical reaction using symbols and formulae. Reactants are on the left side and products on the right side.
Balanced Chemical Equation: Has an equal number of atoms of each element on both sides. Follows the Law of Conservation of Mass.
Law of Conservation of Mass: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed. Total mass of reactants equals total mass of products.
Combination Reaction: Two or more substances combine to form a single product. Example: Calcium and oxygen combine to form calcium oxide.
Decomposition Reaction: One compound breaks into two or more simpler substances. Can occur by heat (thermal), electricity (electrolytic) or sunlight (photolytic).
Displacement Reaction: A more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from its compound. Example: Zinc displaces copper from copper sulphate solution.
Double Displacement Reaction: Exchange of ions between two compounds, often forming a precipitate. Example: Reaction of sodium sulphate and barium chloride.
Precipitate: An insoluble solid formed during a double displacement reaction. Separates from the solution.
Oxidation: Addition of oxygen or removal of hydrogen from a substance. Also involves loss of electrons.
Reduction: Removal of oxygen or addition of hydrogen to a substance. Also involves gain of electrons.
Redox Reaction: Oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously. One substance gets oxidised while the other gets reduced.
Corrosion: Slow destruction of metals due to reaction with air and moisture. Rusting of iron is a common example.
Rust: Hydrated iron oxide formed when iron reacts with oxygen and water. It weakens iron structures.
Galvanisation: Coating iron with zinc to prevent rusting. The zinc layer acts as a protective barrier.
Rancidity: Oxidation of fats and oils in food causing unpleasant smell and taste. Prevented by refrigeration, airtight containers and antioxidants.

flashcards.q: What is a chemical reaction?
flashcards.a: A process in which reactants are converted into new substances called products.

flashcards.q: Why should a chemical equation be balanced?
flashcards.a: To obey the Law of Conservation of Mass.

flashcards.q: What is a combination reaction?
flashcards.a: A reaction in which two or more substances combine to form one product.

flashcards.q: Name the three types of decomposition reactions.
flashcards.a: Thermal, electrolytic and photolytic decomposition.

flashcards.q: What is oxidation?
flashcards.a: Addition of oxygen or removal of hydrogen from a substance.

flashcards.q: What is a redox reaction?
flashcards.a: A reaction in which oxidation and reduction occur simultaneously.

flashcards.q: How can rancidity be prevented?
flashcards.a: By refrigeration, airtight storage, antioxidants and nitrogen packing.

practice.q: Give one example each of combination and decomposition reactions.
practice.a: Combination: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Decomposition: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂ (thermal).

practice.q: What is the difference between displacement and double displacement reactions?
practice.a: In displacement, one element replaces another in a compound. In double displacement, ions of two compounds exchange places.

practice.q: How does galvanisation prevent rusting?
practice.a: Galvanisation coats iron with zinc. Zinc is more reactive and reacts preferentially with air and moisture, protecting the iron underneath.

practice.q: Define corrosion and give one example.
practice.a: Corrosion is the slow destruction of metals due to reaction with the environment. Example: rusting of iron.

practice.q: State the Law of Conservation of Mass.
practice.a: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed during a chemical reaction. Total mass of reactants equals total mass of products.

chapters.title: Effects of Electric Current
notes: Electric Current: The flow of electric charges through a conductor. SI unit is ampere (A). Measured using an ammeter connected in series.
Potential Difference: The work done to move a unit charge between two points. Measured using a voltmeter connected in parallel. SI unit is volt (V).
Resistance: Opposition offered by a conductor to the flow of electric current. SI unit is ohm (Ω). Depends on length, thickness, material and temperature.
Ohm's Law: At constant temperature, current is directly proportional to potential difference. Formula: V = IR.
Series Connection: Resistors have the same current. Equivalent resistance = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ...
Parallel Connection: Resistors have the same potential difference. 1/R = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. Equivalent resistance is less than the smallest individual resistance.
Electric Power: Rate at which electrical energy is consumed. SI unit is watt (W).
Electrical Energy: Measured in kilowatt-hour (kWh). One unit of electricity = one kilowatt-hour.
Heating Effect: Electric current converts electrical energy into heat. Used in heaters, irons and electric kettles.
Joule's Law of Heating: Heat produced is directly proportional to I²Rt. Greater current, resistance or time produces more heat.
Electric Fuse: Protects electrical circuits from excessive current. Melts and breaks the circuit when current exceeds the safe limit.
MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker): Automatically switches off supply during overload or short circuit. Safer and reusable unlike traditional fuses.
Short Circuit: Occurs when live and neutral wires come into direct contact. Causes sudden very large current.
Overloading: Occurs when many appliances draw current from the same circuit. May overheat wires and damage equipment.
Electromagnetic Effect: A current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field. Principle used in electromagnets.
Electromagnet: Produced by passing current through a coil around a soft iron core. Loses magnetism when current is switched off.
Electric Motor: Converts electrical energy into mechanical energy. Works on the magnetic effect of current.
Electric Generator: Converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. Works on the principle of electromagnetic induction.

flashcards.q: What is electric current?
flashcards.a: The flow of electric charges through a conductor. SI unit: ampere (A).

flashcards.q: State Ohm's Law.
flashcards.a: At constant temperature, current is directly proportional to potential difference. V = IR.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of resistance?
flashcards.a: Ohm (Ω).

flashcards.q: State Joule's Law of Heating.
flashcards.a: Heat produced is directly proportional to I²Rt.

flashcards.q: What is the function of an electric fuse?
flashcards.a: It protects electrical circuits from excessive current by melting and breaking the circuit.

flashcards.q: What is an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: A temporary magnet produced by passing current through a coil around a soft iron core.

flashcards.q: What converts electrical energy into mechanical energy?
flashcards.a: An electric motor.

practice.q: What is the difference between series and parallel connections of resistors?
practice.a: In series: same current flows, R_total = R₁+R₂+R₃. In parallel: same voltage, 1/R = 1/R₁+1/R₂+1/R₃.

practice.q: What is a short circuit and what danger does it pose?
practice.a: A short circuit occurs when live and neutral wires touch directly, causing a sudden large current that may damage appliances or cause a fire.

practice.q: How does an electric generator work?
practice.a: An electric generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy using the principle of electromagnetic induction.

practice.q: What is overloading? How can it be prevented?
practice.a: Overloading occurs when too many appliances draw current from the same circuit. Prevented by using MCBs and not connecting too many devices to one socket.

practice.q: Calculate heat produced when a current of 2A flows through a 5Ω resistor for 10 seconds.

chapters.title: Heat
notes: Heat: A form of energy that flows from a hotter body to a colder body. Transfer continues until both bodies reach the same temperature.
Temperature: The measure of the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. Measured in degree Celsius (°C) or Kelvin (K).
Specific Heat Capacity: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C. Different substances have different specific heat capacities.
Water's High Specific Heat Capacity: Water heats up and cools down more slowly than most substances, making it useful as a coolant.
Conduction: Transfer of heat without actual movement of particles. Mainly occurs in solids, especially metals that have free electrons.
Convection: Transfer of heat by actual movement of fluids. Occurs in liquids and gases through convection currents.
Sea Breeze: Caused by convection during daytime. Cool air from the sea moves towards the land because land heats faster.
Land Breeze: Occurs at night when land cools faster. Cool air moves from land towards the sea.
Radiation: Transfer of heat without any material medium. Heat from the Sun reaches Earth through radiation.
Good Absorbers of Heat: Dark coloured and rough surfaces absorb heat more effectively.
Good Reflectors of Heat: Light coloured and polished surfaces reflect heat more effectively. Used in solar cookers and thermos flasks.
Thermos Flask: Reduces heat transfer by conduction, convection and radiation to keep liquids hot or cold for longer.
Expansion: Increase in size of a substance when heated. Solids, liquids and gases all expand on heating.
Anomalous Expansion of Water: Water contracts on heating from 0°C to 4°C and expands above 4°C. Density is maximum at 4°C.
Ice Floats on Water: Because ice has lower density than liquid water, helping aquatic organisms survive in winter.

flashcards.q: What is heat?
flashcards.a: Heat is a form of energy that flows from a hotter body to a colder body.

flashcards.q: Name the three modes of heat transfer.
flashcards.a: Conduction, convection and radiation.

flashcards.q: Which mode of heat transfer does not require a material medium?
flashcards.a: Radiation.

flashcards.q: Why are metals good conductors of heat?
flashcards.a: Because they contain free electrons that transfer heat energy easily.

flashcards.q: What is specific heat capacity?
flashcards.a: The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of a substance by 1°C.

flashcards.q: What is the anomalous expansion of water?
flashcards.a: Water contracts from 0°C to 4°C and expands above 4°C, having maximum density at 4°C.

flashcards.q: What causes sea breeze?
flashcards.a: Convection currents due to unequal heating of land and sea during daytime.

practice.q: What is the difference between conduction and convection?
practice.a: Conduction transfers heat without particle movement (mainly solids). Convection transfers heat through actual particle movement (liquids and gases).

practice.q: Why does ice float on water?
practice.a: Ice is less dense than liquid water because of its open crystal structure. Since density of ice < water, it floats.

practice.q: How does a thermos flask minimise heat transfer?
practice.a: Its double-walled structure with vacuum between walls stops convection and conduction. Silver coating on walls reduces radiation.

practice.q: Why is water used as a coolant in car engines?
practice.a: Water has a very high specific heat capacity, so it absorbs large amounts of heat without a significant rise in temperature.

practice.q: Explain sea breeze and land breeze using the concept of convection.
practice.a: Sea breeze: Land heats faster during day; hot air rises over land, cool sea air moves in. Land breeze: Sea cools slower at night; hot air rises over sea, cool land air moves towards sea.

chapters.title: Refraction of Light
notes: Refraction: The bending of light when it passes from one transparent medium to another. Occurs because the speed of light changes in different media.
Speed of Light: Travels fastest in vacuum. Slows down in other transparent materials. Speed decreases in optically denser media.
Direction of Bending: When light enters a denser medium, it bends towards the normal. When it enters a rarer medium, it bends away from the normal.
Snell's First Law: The incident ray, refracted ray and normal lie in the same plane, perpendicular to the surface of separation.
Snell's Second Law: The ratio of sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant. This constant is the refractive index.
Refractive Index: Indicates how much a medium slows down light. Higher refractive index means optically denser medium. Formula: n = speed of light in vacuum ÷ speed in medium.
Apparent Depth: Apparent depth of water is less than actual depth because of refraction. Objects underwater appear raised.
Pencil in Water: A pencil partly immersed in water appears bent at the surface — a common example of refraction.
Lateral Displacement by Glass Slab: A glass slab causes lateral shift of the emergent ray. The emergent ray remains parallel to the incident ray.
Atmospheric Refraction: Earth's atmosphere has layers of different densities causing several natural optical phenomena.
Stars Twinkle: Due to atmospheric refraction. Constantly changing atmosphere changes the apparent position of stars.
Early Sunrise and Late Sunset: The Sun appears slightly above the horizon before actual sunrise and after actual sunset due to atmospheric refraction.
Mirage: An optical illusion caused by total internal reflection of light in air layers at different temperatures. Commonly seen on hot roads and deserts.
Optical Fibres: Transmit light through repeated total internal reflection. Widely used in communication and medical instruments.
Applications of Refraction: Used in lenses, cameras, microscopes, telescopes and optical fibres.

flashcards.q: What is refraction of light?
flashcards.a: The bending of light when it passes from one transparent medium to another.

flashcards.q: Why does refraction occur?
flashcards.a: Because the speed of light changes in different media.

flashcards.q: State Snell's Second Law.
flashcards.a: The ratio of sine of the angle of incidence to sine of the angle of refraction is constant — this constant is called the refractive index.

flashcards.q: What is refractive index?
flashcards.a: The ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to its speed in a medium.

flashcards.q: Why do stars twinkle?
flashcards.a: Due to atmospheric refraction causing continuous change in the apparent position of stars.

flashcards.q: What is a mirage?
flashcards.a: An optical illusion caused by refraction through layers of air at different temperatures.

flashcards.q: Why does a pencil appear bent in water?
flashcards.a: Due to refraction of light at the water-air boundary.

practice.q: State Snell's two laws of refraction.
practice.a: First: Incident ray, refracted ray and normal are in the same plane. Second: sin i / sin r = constant (refractive index).

practice.q: Why does the Sun appear above the horizon even before it actually rises?
practice.a: Due to atmospheric refraction. Light from the Sun bends as it passes through air layers of increasing density, making the Sun visible before it crosses the horizon.

practice.q: What is the principle of optical fibres?
practice.a: Optical fibres work on total internal reflection. Light entering at the correct angle undergoes repeated total internal reflections and travels along the fibre without escaping.

practice.q: How is the apparent depth of a pond different from its actual depth?
practice.a: Due to refraction, light from objects at the bottom bends away from normal as it exits the water, making the pond appear shallower than it actually is.

practice.q: Name three optical instruments that use refraction.
practice.a: Camera, microscope and telescope.

chapters.title: Lenses
notes: Lens: A transparent optical device bounded by two refracting surfaces. Refracts light to form images of objects.
Convex Lens: Thicker at the centre and thinner at the edges. Converges parallel rays of light to a single point — called a converging lens.
Concave Lens: Thinner at the centre and thicker at the edges. Diverges parallel rays of light — called a diverging lens.
Principal Axis: An imaginary straight line passing through the optical centre of the lens. Used to locate image positions.
Optical Centre: The point through which a light ray passes without deviation. Lies near the centre of the lens.
Principal Focus of Convex Lens: The point where parallel rays meet after refraction. Two principal foci on opposite sides.
Principal Focus of Concave Lens: The point from which parallel rays appear to diverge. Virtual focus on the same side as incident light.
Focal Length: Distance between the optical centre and the principal focus. Represented by f.
Image Formation by Convex Lens: Forms real, inverted images in most cases. Forms virtual, erect and magnified image when object is between optical centre and focus.
Image Formation by Concave Lens: Always forms a virtual, erect and diminished image between optical centre and principal focus.
Lens Formula: Relates object distance (u), image distance (v) and focal length (f). Formula: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u.
Magnification: Ratio of image height to object height. Also equal to image distance divided by object distance.
Power of a Lens: Indicates ability to converge or diverge light. SI unit is dioptre (D).
Power of Convex Lens: Positive, because it converges light.
Power of Concave Lens: Negative, because it diverges light.
Myopia (Short-sightedness): Corrected using a concave lens. Helps distant objects form images on the retina.
Hypermetropia (Long-sightedness): Corrected using a convex lens. Helps nearby objects focus on the retina.
Applications: Cameras, microscopes, telescopes, projectors, spectacles and binoculars.

flashcards.q: What is a lens?
flashcards.a: A transparent optical device bounded by two refracting surfaces that forms images by refracting light.

flashcards.q: Which lens is called a converging lens?
flashcards.a: Convex lens.

flashcards.q: Which lens is called a diverging lens?
flashcards.a: Concave lens.

flashcards.q: State the lens formula.
flashcards.a: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u, where f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance.

flashcards.q: What is the SI unit of power of a lens?
flashcards.a: Dioptre (D).

flashcards.q: Which lens corrects myopia?
flashcards.a: Concave lens.

flashcards.q: Which lens corrects hypermetropia?
flashcards.a: Convex lens.

practice.q: What type of image does a concave lens always form?
practice.a: A concave lens always forms a virtual, erect and diminished image on the same side as the object.

practice.q: What is the difference between a convex and concave lens?
practice.a: A convex lens is thicker at the centre, converges light and can form real images. A concave lens is thinner at the centre, diverges light and always forms virtual images.

practice.q: What is the power of a lens? How is it related to focal length?
practice.a: Power is the ability of a lens to converge or diverge light. P = 1/f (in metres). Unit: dioptre (D).

practice.q: A person cannot see distant objects clearly. Which defect does he have and how is it corrected?
practice.a: He has myopia (short-sightedness). It is corrected using a concave lens of suitable focal length.

practice.q: Name four optical instruments that use lenses.
practice.a: Camera, microscope, telescope and projector.

chapters.title: Metallurgy
notes: Metallurgy: The science of extracting metals from their ores and refining them for use.
Minerals and Ores: Minerals are naturally occurring substances in the Earth's crust. An ore is a mineral from which a metal can be extracted economically.
Reactivity Series: Arranges metals according to their chemical reactivity. Helps in selecting suitable extraction methods.
Highly Reactive Metals: Sodium, aluminium and calcium extracted by electrolysis. Their compounds cannot be reduced using carbon.
Moderately Reactive Metals: Iron and zinc extracted by reduction with carbon. Ores heated with coke or carbon monoxide.
Less Reactive Metals: Mercury and copper obtained by roasting or heating their ores. Easier to extract due to lower reactivity.
Very Low Reactive Metals: Gold and platinum occur in the native state — free metallic form in nature.
Concentration of Ore: Removes unwanted impurities called gangue to increase the percentage of desired metal.
Roasting: Heating sulphide ores in excess air. Converts sulphides into metal oxides.
Calcination: Heating carbonate ores in absence or limited air. Converts carbonates into metal oxides.
Reduction: Converts metal oxides into pure metals using carbon, carbon monoxide or aluminium as reducing agents.
Electrolytic Refining: Used to obtain highly pure metals. Pure metal deposits on the cathode during electrolysis.
Alloys: Homogeneous mixtures of two or more metals or a metal with a non-metal. Possess better properties than pure metals.
Stainless Steel: Alloy of iron, chromium and nickel. Resistant to rusting and corrosion.
Brass: Alloy of copper and zinc. Used for utensils and musical instruments.
Bronze: Alloy of copper and tin. Used for statues, medals and bearings.
Galvanisation: Coating iron with zinc to prevent rusting. Zinc layer prevents contact with air and moisture.
Recycling of Metals: Conserves natural resources, saves energy and reduces environmental pollution.

flashcards.q: What is metallurgy?
flashcards.a: The science of extracting and refining metals from their ores.

flashcards.q: What is an ore?
flashcards.a: A mineral from which a metal can be extracted economically.

flashcards.q: What is gangue?
flashcards.a: Unwanted impurities present in an ore.

flashcards.q: What is roasting?
flashcards.a: Heating sulphide ores in excess air to convert them into oxides.

flashcards.q: Which process is used to extract highly reactive metals?
flashcards.a: Electrolysis.

flashcards.q: What is an alloy?
flashcards.a: A homogeneous mixture of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal.

flashcards.q: What is galvanisation?
flashcards.a: Coating iron with zinc to prevent rusting.

practice.q: Give the composition of stainless steel, brass and bronze.
practice.a: Stainless steel: iron, chromium, nickel. Brass: copper and zinc. Bronze: copper and tin.

practice.q: Why are highly reactive metals extracted by electrolysis?
practice.a: Their compounds are too stable to be reduced by carbon or CO. Electrolysis provides the energy needed to break them down.

practice.q: What is the difference between roasting and calcination?
practice.a: Roasting heats sulphide ores in excess air. Calcination heats carbonate ores in limited or no air.

practice.q: Why is recycling of metals important?
practice.a: It conserves limited natural resources, saves energy required for extraction and reduces environmental pollution.

practice.q: What is electrolytic refining? What is deposited at the cathode?
practice.a: A process to purify metals using electrolysis. Pure metal is deposited at the cathode while impurities collect as anode mud.

chapters.title: Carbon Compounds
notes: Carbon's Uniqueness: Carbon forms a large number of compounds due to tetravalency (four valence electrons, four covalent bonds) and catenation.
Catenation: The property of carbon to form long chains, branched chains or rings with other carbon atoms. Allows formation of millions of compounds.
Covalent Compounds: Carbon mainly forms covalent compounds by sharing electrons. These are generally strong and stable.
Hydrocarbons: Compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen. The simplest organic compounds.
Saturated Hydrocarbons (Alkanes): Contain only single covalent bonds between carbon atoms. Comparatively less reactive. Example: methane (CH₄).
Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Contain one or more double (alkenes) or triple (alkynes) bonds. More reactive than alkanes.
Methane: Simplest saturated hydrocarbon (CH₄). Main component of natural gas and biogas.
Ethene: Unsaturated hydrocarbon with a carbon-carbon double bond. Widely used in manufacture of plastics.
Ethyne (Acetylene): Contains a carbon-carbon triple bond. Used in oxy-acetylene welding.
Functional Groups: Atoms or groups of atoms that determine the chemical properties of organic compounds. Same functional group shows similar chemical behaviour.
Alcohols: Contain the hydroxyl (-OH) functional group. Ethanol is a common alcohol used as a solvent and fuel.
Carboxylic Acids: Contain the carboxyl (-COOH) functional group. Ethanoic acid (acetic acid) is a common example.
Homologous Series: A group of organic compounds with the same functional group. Successive members differ by a –CH₂ unit.
IUPAC Naming: Naming of organic compounds follows systematic IUPAC rules based on carbon count and functional group.
Combustion: Carbon compounds react with oxygen producing CO₂, water and heat.
Esterification: Reaction between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid to produce an ester and water. Catalysed by concentrated H₂SO₄.
Saponification: Alkaline hydrolysis of esters to produce soap and alcohol. Used in soap manufacturing.

flashcards.q: Why does carbon form a large number of compounds?
flashcards.a: Due to tetravalency and catenation.

flashcards.q: What is catenation?
flashcards.a: The property of carbon to form long chains or rings with other carbon atoms.

flashcards.q: What are hydrocarbons?
flashcards.a: Compounds containing only carbon and hydrogen.

flashcards.q: What is the functional group of alcohols?
flashcards.a: Hydroxyl group (-OH).

flashcards.q: What is the functional group of carboxylic acids?
flashcards.a: Carboxyl group (-COOH).

flashcards.q: What is esterification?
flashcards.a: The reaction between an alcohol and a carboxylic acid to form an ester and water.

flashcards.q: What is saponification?
flashcards.a: The reaction of an ester with an alkali to produce soap and alcohol.

practice.q: What is the difference between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons?
practice.a: Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) contain only single bonds and are less reactive. Unsaturated hydrocarbons (alkenes/alkynes) contain double or triple bonds and are more reactive.

practice.q: What is a homologous series? Give an example.
practice.a: A group of compounds with the same functional group where successive members differ by –CH₂. Example: methane (CH₄), ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈) — the alkane series.

practice.q: Write the reaction for esterification of ethanol and acetic acid.
practice.a: CH₃COOH + C₂H₅OH → CH₃COOC₂H₅ + H₂O (in presence of concentrated H₂SO₄)

practice.q: Name two uses of esters.
practice.a: Used as fragrances in perfumes and as flavouring agents in food industries.

practice.q: What are the products of combustion of carbon compounds?
practice.a: Carbon dioxide, water and a large amount of heat energy.

chapters.title: Space Missions
notes: Space Research: Helps scientists understand Earth, planets and the universe. Improves communication, weather forecasting and navigation.
Artificial Satellites: Man-made objects launched into space to revolve around the Earth. Perform scientific and technological functions.
Natural Satellites: Celestial bodies that revolve around planets. The Moon is Earth's natural satellite.
Launch Vehicles (Rockets): Powerful vehicles used to place satellites into orbit. Overcome Earth's gravitational pull.
Geostationary Satellites: Revolve around the Earth in 24 hours and remain fixed above the same equatorial point. Used for communication and TV broadcasting.
Polar Satellites: Revolve around the Earth from pole to pole. Used for mapping, weather forecasting and remote sensing.
Remote Sensing Satellites: Collect information about Earth's surface from space. Used in agriculture, disaster management and environmental studies.
Communication Satellites: Transmit television, radio, telephone and internet signals. Have revolutionised global communication.
Navigation Satellites: Provide accurate position and timing. India's NavIC system supports navigation across the country.
ISRO: The Indian Space Research Organisation — India's national space agency. Has achieved many successful space missions at low cost.
PSLV: Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle — one of ISRO's most successful launch vehicles for polar orbits.
GSLV: Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle — used for heavier satellites in geostationary orbits. Uses indigenous cryogenic engine technology.
Chandrayaan-1: India's first Moon mission. Discovered evidence of water molecules on the lunar surface.
Chandrayaan-2: Aimed to study Moon's surface, minerals and atmosphere. Orbiter continues providing scientific data.
Chandrayaan-3: Successfully achieved a soft landing near Moon's south pole. Made India the first country to land there and fourth to achieve soft lunar landing.
Mangalyaan (Mars Orbiter Mission): Successfully reached Mars in its first attempt. India became the first Asian nation to achieve this milestone.
Space Technology Applications: Supports disaster management, communication, national security and climate change monitoring.

flashcards.q: What is an artificial satellite?
flashcards.a: A man-made object launched into space to revolve around the Earth.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between a geostationary and a polar satellite?
flashcards.a: A geostationary satellite remains fixed above the same equatorial point, while a polar satellite passes over both poles.

flashcards.q: What is ISRO?
flashcards.a: The Indian Space Research Organisation — India's national space agency.

flashcards.q: Which mission discovered water molecules on the Moon?
flashcards.a: Chandrayaan-1.

flashcards.q: What was the objective of Mangalyaan?
flashcards.a: To study the planet Mars.

flashcards.q: What is NavIC?
flashcards.a: India's regional satellite navigation system.

flashcards.q: What is the full form of PSLV?
flashcards.a: Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle.

practice.q: Name the three Chandrayaan missions and their key achievements.
practice.a: Chandrayaan-1: discovered water on Moon. Chandrayaan-2: orbiter studying Moon. Chandrayaan-3: first soft landing near Moon's south pole.

practice.q: Name two types of satellites and their uses.
practice.a: Geostationary satellites: TV and communication. Polar satellites: weather forecasting and remote sensing.

practice.q: Why is Mangalyaan considered a milestone for India?
practice.a: India's Mangalyaan reached Mars in its first attempt, making India the first Asian nation to achieve a successful Mars mission.

practice.q: Name three uses of remote sensing satellites.
practice.a: Agriculture monitoring, disaster management and environmental studies.

practice.q: Why are space missions important for daily life?
practice.a: They improve communication, enable weather forecasting, support GPS navigation and help in disaster management.

chapters.title: Heredity and Evolution
notes: Heredity: The transmission of traits from parents to offspring. Ensures living organisms resemble their parents.
Variations: Differences seen among individuals of the same species. Help organisms adapt to changing conditions. Only inherited variations are passed to the next generation.
Gregor Johann Mendel: Known as the Father of Genetics. Explained basic principles of heredity through pea plant experiments.
Traits and Genes: A trait is a specific characteristic controlled by genes. Genes are functional units of heredity present on chromosomes.
Chromosomes and DNA: Chromosomes are thread-like structures in the nucleus made of DNA and proteins. DNA stores hereditary information.
Alleles: Alternative forms of the same gene. Determine different forms of a trait.
Dominant Trait: Expressed even with one dominant allele. Masks the recessive trait in the F₁ generation.
Recessive Trait: Expressed only when both alleles are recessive.
Mendel's Law of Dominance: Dominant traits mask recessive traits — only dominant traits appear in the F₁ generation.
Mendel's Law of Segregation: Allele pairs separate during gamete formation. Each gamete receives only one allele of each gene.
Monohybrid Cross: Inheritance of one pair of contrasting characters. Demonstrates Mendel's basic laws. F₂ ratio is 3:1 (dominant:recessive).
Phenotype: The observable appearance of an organism.
Genotype: The genetic makeup of an organism.
Sex Determination in Humans: Females have XX chromosomes; males have XY chromosomes. Father determines sex of child by contributing X or Y.
Evolution: Gradual change in living organisms over millions of years. Leads to formation of new species.
Natural Selection: Proposed by Charles Darwin. Organisms with favourable variations survive and reproduce more successfully.
Fossils: Preserved remains or impressions of ancient organisms. Provide evidence for evolution.
Homologous Organs: Similar structure, different functions — indicate common ancestry.
Analogous Organs: Different structures, similar functions — evidence of convergent evolution.

flashcards.q: What is heredity?
flashcards.a: The transmission of traits from parents to offspring.

flashcards.q: Who is called the Father of Genetics?
flashcards.a: Gregor Johann Mendel.

flashcards.q: What is a gene?
flashcards.a: The functional unit of heredity present on chromosomes.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between genotype and phenotype?
flashcards.a: Genotype is the genetic makeup. Phenotype is the observable appearance.

flashcards.q: Who determines the sex of the child?
flashcards.a: The father, by contributing either an X or Y chromosome.

flashcards.q: What are fossils?
flashcards.a: Preserved remains or impressions of ancient organisms.

flashcards.q: What is natural selection?
flashcards.a: The process by which organisms with favourable variations survive and reproduce successfully.

practice.q: State Mendel's Law of Segregation.
practice.a: Allele pairs separate during gamete formation so each gamete receives only one allele for each gene.

practice.q: What is a monohybrid cross? What ratio does it give in F₂?
practice.a: A monohybrid cross involves one pair of contrasting characters. F₂ gives a 3:1 ratio of dominant to recessive traits.

practice.q: What is the difference between homologous and analogous organs?
practice.a: Homologous organs have similar structure but different functions, indicating common ancestry. Analogous organs have different structures but similar functions.

practice.q: Explain how sex is determined in humans.
practice.a: Females are XX and males are XY. The mother always contributes X. If the father contributes X, the child is female (XX). If Y, the child is male (XY).

practice.q: What evidence do fossils provide for evolution?
practice.a: Fossils show the preserved remains of organisms from different geological periods, demonstrating gradual changes in life forms over millions of years.

chapters.title: Life Processes in Living Organisms
notes: Life Processes: Basic activities necessary for survival — nutrition, respiration, transportation and excretion.
Nutrition: Process by which organisms obtain and use food for energy, growth and repair.
Autotrophs (Photosynthesis): Plants prepare food using sunlight, CO₂ and water in chloroplasts. Produce glucose and oxygen.
Heterotrophs: Cannot prepare own food. Depend on other organisms. Include animals, fungi and most microorganisms.
Human Digestive System: Mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine. Breaks complex food into absorbable nutrients.
Mouth: Digestion begins with saliva acting on starch.
Stomach: Secretes hydrochloric acid and enzymes. Digests proteins and kills harmful microorganisms.
Small Intestine: Most digestion and absorption occur here. Villi increase the surface area for absorption.
Respiration: Process of releasing energy from food inside cells. Produces ATP.
Aerobic Respiration: In presence of oxygen. Releases large amount of energy. Products: CO₂ and water.
Anaerobic Respiration: Without oxygen. Releases less energy. Products differ (e.g. lactic acid in muscles, CO₂ and ethanol in yeast).
Human Respiratory System: Nose, trachea, lungs and diaphragm. Alveoli are tiny air sacs for gaseous exchange — large surface area.
Circulatory System: Heart, arteries, veins and blood. Transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones and removes waste.
Heart: Four-chambered muscular organ that pumps blood continuously.
Blood Components: Plasma, red blood cells (carry oxygen), white blood cells (immunity) and platelets (clotting).
Excretory System: Removes metabolic wastes — kidneys, ureters, urinary bladder and urethra.
Kidneys: Filter blood and produce urine. Remove excess water, salts and nitrogenous wastes.
Nephrons: Functional units of the kidneys. Filter blood and regulate body fluid composition.

flashcards.q: What are life processes?
flashcards.a: Basic activities necessary for the survival of living organisms: nutrition, respiration, transportation and excretion.

flashcards.q: What is photosynthesis?
flashcards.a: The process by which green plants prepare food using sunlight, CO₂ and water in chloroplasts.

flashcards.q: Where does most digestion and absorption take place?
flashcards.a: In the small intestine.

flashcards.q: What are villi?
flashcards.a: Finger-like projections in the small intestine that increase the surface area for absorption.

flashcards.q: What are alveoli?
flashcards.a: Tiny air sacs in the lungs where gaseous exchange occurs.

flashcards.q: What is the functional unit of the kidney?
flashcards.a: Nephron.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
flashcards.a: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen and releases more energy. Anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen and releases less energy.

practice.q: Write the equation for photosynthesis.
practice.a: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (in presence of sunlight and chlorophyll)

practice.q: What is the role of hydrochloric acid in the stomach?
practice.a: HCl creates an acidic medium for digestive enzymes to work and also kills harmful microorganisms in food.

practice.q: Name the four components of blood and their functions.
practice.a: Plasma (liquid medium), RBCs (carry oxygen), WBCs (immunity) and platelets (blood clotting).

practice.q: What is the role of the kidneys?
practice.a: Kidneys filter blood, remove nitrogenous wastes and excess salts and water, and produce urine to maintain the body's internal balance.

practice.q: Differentiate between arteries and veins.
practice.a: Arteries carry blood away from the heart at high pressure. Veins return blood to the heart and contain valves to prevent backflow.

chapters.title: Environmental Management
notes: Environment: Includes all living and non-living components surrounding us. Provides resources necessary for survival.
Environmental Management: Planned use and conservation of natural resources to maintain ecological balance for present and future generations.
Natural Resources: Classified as renewable (can be replenished) and non-renewable (limited in nature). Conservation prevents their depletion.
Biodiversity: Variety of living organisms on Earth. Maintains ecosystem stability. Depletion leads to ecological imbalance.
Deforestation: Large-scale cutting of forests. Causes soil erosion, habitat loss and climate change.
Afforestation: Planting trees in areas where forests have been destroyed. Restores ecological balance.
Wildlife Conservation: National parks, wildlife sanctuaries and biosphere reserves protect endangered species.
Air Pollution: Smoke, vehicle emissions and industrial gases. Causes respiratory diseases and global warming.
Water Pollution: Sewage, industrial waste and agricultural chemicals. Makes water unsafe for drinking and aquatic life.
Soil Pollution: Excessive pesticides, fertilizers and waste. Reduces soil fertility.
Noise Pollution: Loud sounds from vehicles, industries and loudspeakers. Causes hearing loss and stress.
Solid Waste Management: Proper collection, segregation and disposal. Recycling and composting reduce pollution.
3Rs: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle — conserve resources and minimize waste.
Rainwater Harvesting: Collecting and storing rainwater. Recharges groundwater and conserves water.
Watershed Management: Conservation of soil and water. Reduces erosion and supports agriculture.
Climate Change: Caused by increased concentration of greenhouse gases. Results in global warming and changing weather patterns.
Sustainable Development: Meets present needs without harming future generations. Balances economic growth with environmental conservation.

flashcards.q: What is environmental management?
flashcards.a: The planned use and conservation of natural resources to maintain ecological balance.

flashcards.q: What is biodiversity?
flashcards.a: The variety of living organisms found on Earth.

flashcards.q: What is deforestation?
flashcards.a: The large-scale cutting down of forests.

flashcards.q: What are the 3Rs of waste management?
flashcards.a: Reduce, Reuse and Recycle.

flashcards.q: What is rainwater harvesting?
flashcards.a: The collection and storage of rainwater for future use.

flashcards.q: What is sustainable development?
flashcards.a: Development that meets present needs without affecting the ability of future generations to meet theirs.

flashcards.q: What is afforestation?
flashcards.a: Planting trees to restore forest cover and ecological balance.

practice.q: Name four major types of pollution and one cause of each.
practice.a: Air: vehicle emissions. Water: sewage. Soil: pesticides. Noise: heavy traffic.

practice.q: How does deforestation contribute to climate change?
practice.a: Trees absorb CO₂. Removing them increases CO₂ levels in the atmosphere, intensifying the greenhouse effect and causing global warming.

practice.q: What is the role of national parks and wildlife sanctuaries?
practice.a: They provide protected habitats for endangered animals and plants, helping prevent extinction and preserving biodiversity.

practice.q: Why is watershed management important?
practice.a: It conserves soil and water, reduces erosion, supports agriculture and helps maintain local water availability.

practice.q: Suggest three ways an individual can help protect the environment.
practice.a: Plant trees, use reusable bags instead of plastic and conserve water and electricity at home.

chapters.title: Towards Green Energy
notes: Green Energy: Energy produced from renewable and environmentally friendly sources. Reduces pollution and conserves natural resources.
Conventional Energy Sources: Coal, petroleum and natural gas. Limited, non-renewable and cause environmental pollution.
Non-conventional Energy Sources: Renewable and eco-friendly. Include solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal, geothermal and biomass energy.
Solar Energy: Obtained from the Sun using solar panels and photovoltaic cells. Clean, renewable and widely available.
Photovoltaic Cell: Converts sunlight directly into electrical energy. Used in calculators, street lights and rooftop solar panels.
Wind Energy: Generated by wind turbines converting kinetic energy of air into electricity. Requires suitable windy locations.
Hydroelectric Power: Generated using flowing or falling water. One of the major renewable energy sources globally.
Biomass Energy: Obtained from plant and animal waste. Converted into biogas, biofuels and electricity.
Biogas: Mainly composed of methane. Produced by decomposition of organic waste in biogas plants.
Geothermal Energy: Obtained from heat inside the Earth. Used to generate electricity in volcanic regions.
Tidal Energy: Produced using rise and fall of ocean tides. Renewable but needs suitable coastal conditions.
Hydrogen as Clean Fuel: Produces only water on combustion. Great potential for future energy needs.
Fuel Cells: Generate electricity through chemical reactions without burning fuel. Hydrogen fuel cells are highly efficient.
Electric Vehicles (EVs): Use rechargeable batteries instead of petrol or diesel. Reduce air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions.
Hybrid Vehicles: Use both internal combustion engine and electric motor. Improve fuel efficiency and reduce emissions.
Energy Conservation: Using energy wisely and avoiding wastage. Energy-efficient appliances reduce consumption.
India's Green Energy Initiatives: Solar parks, wind farms and electric mobility schemes promote clean energy goals.

flashcards.q: What is green energy?
flashcards.a: Energy produced from renewable and environmentally friendly sources.

flashcards.q: Name four renewable energy sources.
flashcards.a: Solar, wind, hydroelectric and biomass energy.

flashcards.q: What is a photovoltaic cell?
flashcards.a: A device that converts sunlight directly into electricity.

flashcards.q: What is biogas mainly composed of?
flashcards.a: Methane.

flashcards.q: Why is hydrogen called a clean fuel?
flashcards.a: Because it produces only water during combustion, without any harmful emissions.

flashcards.q: What is the advantage of electric vehicles?
flashcards.a: They reduce air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions.

flashcards.q: What is energy conservation?
flashcards.a: The careful and efficient use of energy to avoid unnecessary wastage.

practice.q: What is the difference between conventional and non-conventional energy sources?
practice.a: Conventional sources (coal, petroleum) are non-renewable and polluting. Non-conventional sources (solar, wind) are renewable and eco-friendly.

practice.q: How does a photovoltaic cell work?
practice.a: A photovoltaic cell converts sunlight directly into electrical energy through the photovoltaic effect in semiconductor materials.

practice.q: What is biogas? How is it produced?
practice.a: Biogas is mainly methane, produced by the decomposition of organic waste (plant and animal matter) by bacteria in biogas plants under anaerobic conditions.

practice.q: Why should India shift towards renewable energy?
practice.a: India's fossil fuel reserves are limited. Renewable sources reduce pollution, lower carbon emissions and provide sustainable long-term energy security.

practice.q: Name two ways to conserve energy at home.
practice.a: Switch off lights and appliances when not in use, and use energy-efficient (star-rated) appliances.

chapters.title: Animal Classification
notes: Classification of Animals: Based on body structure and characteristics. Helps in scientific study and identification.
Invertebrates: Animals without a vertebral column. Make up the majority of animal species.
Vertebrates: Animals with a vertebral column and internal skeleton. Well-developed nervous system and complex organisation.
Phylum Porifera: Simplest multicellular animals (sponges). Have numerous pores for water circulation.
Phylum Coelenterata: Radial symmetry and stinging cells. Examples: Hydra and jellyfish.
Phylum Platyhelminthes: Flatworms with bilaterally symmetrical bodies. Many are parasitic.
Phylum Aschelminthes: Cylindrical worms with a complete digestive system. Example: roundworms.
Phylum Annelida: Segmented worms with true body cavity. Examples: earthworm and leech.
Phylum Arthropoda: The largest phylum. Jointed appendages and hard exoskeleton. Includes insects, spiders and crabs.
Phylum Mollusca: Soft-bodied animals usually with a shell. Examples: snails, octopus and oysters.
Phylum Echinodermata: Marine animals with spiny skin. Examples: starfish and sea urchins.
Phylum Chordata: Animals possessing a notochord at some stage. Vertebrates belong to this phylum.
Pisces (Fish): Aquatic vertebrates, breathe through gills, covered with scales, move with fins.
Amphibia: Live on land and in water. Examples: frog and salamander.
Reptilia: Dry, scaly skin, lay eggs with protective shells. Examples: snakes, lizards and crocodiles.
Aves (Birds): Feathers, forelimbs as wings, warm-blooded. Most can fly.
Mammalia: Possess mammary glands, produce milk for young. Examples: humans, whales and bats.
Importance of Classification: Helps understand evolutionary relationships and simplifies the study of biodiversity.

flashcards.q: What is the main difference between vertebrates and invertebrates?
flashcards.a: Vertebrates have a backbone (vertebral column), while invertebrates do not.

flashcards.q: Which is the largest phylum in the animal kingdom?
flashcards.a: Arthropoda.

flashcards.q: Give two examples of Mollusca.
flashcards.a: Snail and octopus.

flashcards.q: Which phylum does the earthworm belong to?
flashcards.a: Annelida.

flashcards.q: What are the characteristics of birds (Aves)?
flashcards.a: They have feathers, forelimbs modified into wings and are warm-blooded.

flashcards.q: What is the characteristic feature of mammals?
flashcards.a: Presence of mammary glands that produce milk for their young.

flashcards.q: Which animals belong to Echinodermata?
flashcards.a: Starfish and sea urchins.

practice.q: Arrange the following in correct classification: Arthropoda, Annelida, Porifera, Chordata, Mollusca — from simplest to most complex.
practice.a: Porifera → Annelida → Mollusca → Arthropoda → Chordata.

practice.q: What are the key differences between amphibians and reptiles?
practice.a: Amphibians live on land and in water, have moist skin and eggs without shells. Reptiles live on land, have dry scaly skin and eggs with protective shells.

practice.q: Name the five classes of vertebrates.
practice.a: Pisces, Amphibia, Reptilia, Aves and Mammalia.

practice.q: What are the characteristics of Arthropoda?
practice.a: Jointed appendages, hard external skeleton (exoskeleton) and segmented body. Largest phylum — includes insects, spiders, crabs.

practice.q: Why is animal classification important?
practice.a: It helps study evolutionary relationships, identify species and organise the enormous diversity of animal life systematically.

chapters.title: Introduction to Microbiology
notes: Microbiology: The branch of science that studies microorganisms — organisms too small to be seen with the naked eye.
Groups of Microorganisms: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses. Each group has unique structural and functional characteristics.
Bacteria: Unicellular prokaryotic organisms without a true nucleus. Reproduce mainly by binary fission. Classified as coccus, bacillus, spirillum and vibrio by shape.
Useful Bacteria: Used in making curd, cheese and antibiotics (e.g. Lactobacillus, Streptomyces).
Harmful Bacteria: Cause diseases such as cholera (Vibrio cholerae), typhoid and tuberculosis.
Protozoa: Unicellular eukaryotic organisms with a true nucleus. Examples: Amoeba, Paramecium, Plasmodium.
Plasmodium: The protozoan that causes malaria.
Fungi: Eukaryotic organisms absorbing nutrients from organic matter. Reproduce through spores. Grow rapidly in moist conditions.
Yeast: Useful fungus used in baking and fermentation.
Algae: Contain chlorophyll and perform photosynthesis. Produce large amounts of oxygen in aquatic ecosystems.
Viruses: Extremely small infectious particles. Reproduce only inside living cells. Contain DNA or RNA in a protein coat. Considered to lie between living and non-living.
Antibiotics: Medicines produced by certain microorganisms (e.g. fungi) to kill harmful bacteria. Penicillin was the first antibiotic, discovered by Alexander Fleming.
Vaccines: Prepare the body's immune system to fight pathogens. Provide immunity against infectious diseases.
Microorganisms in Agriculture: Used as biofertilizers and biopesticides. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria improve soil fertility.
Nitrogen-fixing Bacteria: Convert atmospheric nitrogen into usable forms for plants, maintaining the nitrogen cycle.
Decomposers: Some microorganisms decompose dead plants and animals, recycling nutrients back into the environment.
Prevention of Microbial Infections: Proper hygiene, sterilization, safe food and clean water reduce spread of microbial diseases.

flashcards.q: What is microbiology?
flashcards.a: The branch of science that studies microorganisms.

flashcards.q: Name the five major groups of microorganisms.
flashcards.a: Bacteria, fungi, protozoa, algae and viruses.

flashcards.q: How do bacteria reproduce?
flashcards.a: By binary fission.

flashcards.q: Which microorganism causes malaria?
flashcards.a: Plasmodium.

flashcards.q: Who discovered penicillin?
flashcards.a: Alexander Fleming.

flashcards.q: Why are viruses called obligate parasites?
flashcards.a: Because they can reproduce only inside living cells.

flashcards.q: What is the role of nitrogen-fixing bacteria?
flashcards.a: They convert atmospheric nitrogen into forms usable by plants.

practice.q: Give two examples each of useful and harmful bacteria.
practice.a: Useful: Lactobacillus (curd), Rhizobium (nitrogen fixation). Harmful: Vibrio cholerae (cholera), Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB).

practice.q: What is the difference between bacteria and viruses?
practice.a: Bacteria are unicellular living organisms that can reproduce independently. Viruses are non-cellular particles that reproduce only inside living host cells.

practice.q: What is the role of yeast?
practice.a: Yeast is used in baking (causes bread to rise by producing CO₂ during fermentation) and in fermentation to make alcoholic beverages.

practice.q: How do biofertilizers improve soil fertility?
practice.a: Biofertilizers contain nitrogen-fixing and other beneficial bacteria that add nutrients to the soil naturally, reducing the need for chemical fertilizers.

practice.q: How do vaccines provide immunity?
practice.a: Vaccines contain weakened or dead pathogens that stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies, preparing it to fight the actual infection.

chapters.title: Cell Biology and Biotechnology
notes: Cell: The basic structural and functional unit of life. All living organisms are made of one or more cells.
Discovery: Robert Hooke first observed cells in 1665 examining cork. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed living cells — called the Father of Microbiology.
Prokaryotic Cells: Lack a true nucleus. No membrane-bound organelles. Example: bacteria.
Eukaryotic Cells: Possess a membrane-bound nucleus and complex organelles. Example: plant and animal cells.
Cell Membrane: Surrounds the cell and controls movement of substances. Selectively permeable.
Nucleus: Controls all cellular activities and contains genetic material. Acts as the control centre.
Cytoplasm: Jelly-like substance inside the cell membrane containing cell organelles.
Mitochondria: Powerhouse of the cell. Produce energy (ATP) through respiration.
Ribosomes: Sites of protein synthesis. May be free in cytoplasm or attached to ER.
Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER): Transports materials and helps in synthesis of proteins and lipids.
Golgi Apparatus: Modifies, packages and secretes cellular products. Also forms lysosomes.
Lysosomes: Contain digestive enzymes that break down worn-out cell parts. Called suicide bags of the cell.
Chloroplasts: Present in plant cells. Carry out photosynthesis. Contain chlorophyll.
Cell Wall: Rigid layer outside cell membrane in plant cells. Provides support and shape.
Cell Division — Mitosis: Produces two genetically identical daughter cells. Helps in growth and tissue repair.
Cell Division — Meiosis: Produces four genetically different cells with half the chromosome number. Occurs during gamete formation.
Biotechnology: Use of living organisms or their products for human welfare. Applications in agriculture, medicine and industry.
Genetic Engineering: Modifying the DNA of organisms to obtain desirable traits. Led to development of GM crops.
Tissue Culture: Growing plant cells under sterile lab conditions. Produces disease-free plants in large numbers.
Biotechnology in Medicine: Used to produce vaccines, insulin and antibiotics, greatly improving healthcare.

flashcards.q: What is a cell?
flashcards.a: The basic structural and functional unit of life.

flashcards.q: Who discovered cells?
flashcards.a: Robert Hooke in 1665.

flashcards.q: Why are mitochondria called the powerhouse of the cell?
flashcards.a: Because they produce ATP (energy) through respiration.

flashcards.q: What is the function of ribosomes?
flashcards.a: Protein synthesis.

flashcards.q: Why are lysosomes called suicide bags?
flashcards.a: Because they contain enzymes that can break down the entire cell if it is damaged.

flashcards.q: What is mitosis?
flashcards.a: Cell division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells for growth and repair.

flashcards.q: What is biotechnology?
flashcards.a: The use of living organisms or their products for human welfare.

practice.q: What is the difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
practice.a: Prokaryotic cells have no membrane-bound nucleus (e.g. bacteria). Eukaryotic cells have a true nucleus and complex organelles (e.g. plant, animal cells).

practice.q: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?
practice.a: Mitosis: produces 2 identical cells, for growth and repair. Meiosis: produces 4 genetically different cells with half the chromosome number, for gamete formation.

practice.q: What is the role of the Golgi apparatus?
practice.a: The Golgi apparatus modifies, packages and secretes cellular products and also forms lysosomes.

practice.q: What is genetic engineering? Give one application.
practice.a: The modification of an organism's DNA to obtain desired traits. Application: producing human insulin using bacteria for diabetic patients.

practice.q: What are the advantages of tissue culture?
practice.a: Produces many identical, disease-free plants rapidly from a single parent plant under sterile lab conditions.

chapters.title: Social Health
notes: Health: A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being. A healthy person functions efficiently in society.
Social Health: The ability to maintain healthy relationships with others and participate positively in society.
Three Dimensions of Health: Physical health, mental health and social health together define overall well-being.
Family and Social Development: Family is the first institution influencing social behaviour. Provides emotional support, values and security.
Role of Friends and Teachers: Positive relationships with friends and teachers improve confidence and emotional well-being.
Communication: Good communication avoids misunderstandings. Listening carefully and expressing thoughts respectfully are key social skills.
Stress: The body's response to physical or emotional challenges. Long-term stress affects both physical and mental health.
Stress Management: Through exercise, hobbies, meditation and proper time management. Seeking guidance from trusted adults also helps.
Addiction: Harmful dependence on substances or unhealthy habits. Tobacco, alcohol and drugs severely damage health and affect family and society.
Substance Abuse: Affects brain, heart, lungs and other organs. Also impacts social relationships and mental health.
Peer Pressure: Influence of friends or a group on an individual's behaviour. Developing self-confidence helps resist negative influences.
Adolescence: A period of rapid physical, emotional and social changes. Understanding these helps teenagers maintain good health.
Healthy Habits During Adolescence: Balanced nutrition, regular exercise, adequate sleep and personal hygiene.
Mental Health: As important as physical health. Positive thinking and emotional balance improve quality of life.
Communicable Diseases: Spread from person to person. Vaccination and hygiene help prevent their spread.
Non-communicable Diseases: Not infectious but result from lifestyle factors. Healthy habits reduce their risk.
Social Awareness and Gender Equality: Social awareness prevents discrimination. Gender equality promotes equal rights, opportunities and respect.
Digital Responsibility: Technology should be used responsibly. Cyberbullying and misuse of social media should be avoided.
Community Service: Volunteering and community service strengthen social responsibility. Build compassion and cooperation.
Balanced Lifestyle: Maintaining physical, mental and social health leads to a balanced and productive life.

flashcards.q: What is social health?
flashcards.a: The ability to maintain healthy relationships and function effectively in society.

flashcards.q: What are the three dimensions of health?
flashcards.a: Physical, mental and social health.

flashcards.q: What is stress?
flashcards.a: The body's response to physical or emotional challenges.

flashcards.q: What is addiction?
flashcards.a: Harmful dependence on substances or unhealthy habits.

flashcards.q: What is adolescence?
flashcards.a: The stage of rapid physical, emotional and social development.

flashcards.q: Why is mental health important?
flashcards.a: It helps maintain emotional balance and overall well-being.

flashcards.q: Why is gender equality important?
flashcards.a: It ensures equal rights, opportunities and respect for everyone.

practice.q: Name three ways to manage stress effectively.
practice.a: Exercise regularly, practise meditation and manage time effectively to avoid pressure.

practice.q: How can peer pressure affect young people? How can it be resisted?
practice.a: Peer pressure can lead teens to make unhealthy choices. It can be resisted by developing self-confidence, thinking critically and seeking guidance from trusted adults.

practice.q: What is the difference between communicable and non-communicable diseases?
practice.a: Communicable diseases spread from person to person (e.g. influenza). Non-communicable diseases are not infectious but result from lifestyle (e.g. diabetes, heart disease).

practice.q: How can social media use affect social health?
practice.a: Overuse or misuse of social media can lead to cyberbullying, isolation and anxiety. Responsible use helps maintain healthy digital relationships.

practice.q: Why is adolescence an important period of life?
practice.a: It involves rapid physical, emotional and social changes. Making healthy choices during this period lays the foundation for long-term physical and mental well-being.

chapters.title: Linear Equations in Two Variables
notes: Simultaneous Equations: Two linear equations in two variables considered together are called simultaneous equations. Their common solution is the pair of x and y values that satisfies both equations.
Graphical Method: Each linear equation represents a straight line on the Cartesian plane. The point where the two lines intersect gives the solution of the simultaneous equations.

Method of Elimination: In this method, one variable is eliminated by making its coefficients equal (and opposite) in both equations, then adding or subtracting the equations. The resulting single-variable equation is solved and back-substituted to find the other variable.
Method of Substitution: One variable is expressed in terms of the other from one equation and substituted into the second equation. This reduces the problem to a linear equation in a single variable.

Determinant Method (Cramer's Rule): For equations a1x+b1y=c1 and a2x+b2y=c2, three determinants are formed: D = a1b2−a2b1, Dx = c1b2−c2b1, Dy = a1c2−a2c1. The solution is x = Dx/D and y = Dy/D, provided D ≠ 0.
Consistency of Equations: If D ≠ 0 the equations have a unique solution (consistent, intersecting lines). If D = 0 and Dx = Dy = 0, the equations have infinitely many solutions (coincident lines).
Inconsistent Equations: If D = 0 but Dx ≠ 0 or Dy ≠ 0, the pair of equations has no solution (inconsistent, parallel lines). This case must always be checked before applying Cramer's rule blindly.

Word Problems: Simultaneous equations are used to solve real-life problems involving two unknowns, such as ages, numbers, speed-distance-time, and cost problems. The problem statement must first be converted into two linear equations before solving.

flashcards.q: What is the determinant method also called?
flashcards.a: Cramer's Rule.

flashcards.q: When do simultaneous equations have no solution?
flashcards.a: When D = 0 but Dx ≠ 0 or Dy ≠ 0.

flashcards.q: When do simultaneous equations have infinitely many solutions?
flashcards.a: When D = 0 and both Dx = 0 and Dy = 0.

flashcards.q: What does the point of intersection of two lines represent graphically?
flashcards.a: The solution of the simultaneous equations.

flashcards.q: Write the formula for D in equations a1x+b1y=c1, a2x+b2y=c2.
flashcards.a: D = a1b2 − a2b1.

practice.q: Solve the following simultaneous equations using Cramer's Rule: 3x − 4y = 10; 4x + 3y = 5.
Solve by the method of elimination: 2x + 3y = 9; 3x − 4y = 5.
practice.a:
Multiply 2x+3y=9 by 4 → 8x+12y=36;
multiply 3x−4y=5 by 3 → 9x−12y=15.
Adding: 17x=51
⟹ x=3.
Substituting in 2x+3y=9: 6+3y=9
⟹ y=1.
Answer: x = 3, y = 1.

practice.q: Find the value of k for which the equations 2x + 3y = 7 and (k+1)x + (2k−1)y = 4k+1 are inconsistent.
practice.a:
D = 2(2k−1) − (k+1)(3) = k−5;
Dx = 7(2k−1) − (4k+1)(3) = 2k−10 = 2(k−5);
Dy = 2(4k+1) − (k+1)(7) = k−5.
Setting D=0 gives k=5, but at k=5, Dx and Dy also become 0 (since both are multiples of k−5).
This means the two equations become identical lines (coincident), giving infinitely many solutions rather than no solution.
Answer: There is no real value of k for which this particular pair of equations becomes inconsistent — at k = 5 the lines coincide (infinite solutions) instead.

practice.q: The sum of digits of a two-digit number is 9. Nine times this number is twice the number obtained by interchanging the digits. Find the number, forming simultaneous equations and solving them.
practice.a:
Let ten's digit = x, unit's digit = y.
Given x+y=9.
Original number =10x+y;
number with digits reversed =10y+x.
Given: 9(10x+y) = 2(10y+x)
⟹ 90x+9y = 20y+2x
⟹ 88x = 11y
⟹ y = 8x.
Substituting in x+y=9: x+8x=9
⟹ x=1, y=8.
Answer: The number is 18. (Check: sum of digits =9;
nine times 18=162;
number reversed =81, twice 81=162 ✓)

practice.q: A two-digit number is such that the product of its digits is 8. When 18 is added to the number, the digits interchange their places. Find the number.
practice.a:
Let ten's digit = x, unit's digit = y.
Given xy = 8.
Since the number increases by 18 on reversing digits: (10x+y)+18 = 10y+x
⟹ 9x−9y = −18
⟹ y = x+2.
Substituting in xy=8: x(x+2)=8
⟹ x²+2x−8=0
⟹ (x+4)(x−2)=0
⟹ x=2 (rejecting x=−4).
So y=4.
Answer: The number is 24. (Check: product of digits = 2×4=8;
24+18=42, which is 24 with digits reversed ✓)

chapters.title: Quadratic Equations
notes: Standard Form: A quadratic equation in variable x is written as ax² + bx + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a ≠ 0. The highest power of the variable in a quadratic equation is 2.

Solution by Factorization: A quadratic expression is factorized into two linear factors, and each factor is equated to zero to get the roots. This method works only when the expression can be neatly factorized.
Solution by Completing the Square: The quadratic equation is rearranged so that one side becomes a perfect square trinomial. This method is the basis for deriving the general quadratic formula.
Quadratic Formula: For ax² + bx + c = 0, the roots are given by x = [−b ± √(b² − 4ac)] / 2a. This formula can solve any quadratic equation directly once a, b, c are identified.
Discriminant: The discriminant of a quadratic equation is Δ = b² − 4ac. It decides the nature of the roots without actually solving the equation.
Nature of Roots: If Δ > 0, the roots are real and unequal; if Δ = 0, the roots are real and equal; if Δ < 0, the roots are not real. This rule helps classify solutions of word problems quickly.
Relation between Roots and Coefficients: If α and β are roots of ax² + bx + c = 0, then α + β = −b/a and α·β = c/a. These relations are used to frame new equations or verify given roots.

Forming a Quadratic Equation from Roots: If the sum and product of the roots are known, the equation is written as x² − (sum of roots)x + (product of roots) = 0. This is the reverse process of finding roots.

Word Problems: Many real-life situations such as numbers, areas, speed and time lead to quadratic equations. The equation must be framed carefully and only the valid root(s), fitting the context, must be accepted as the answer.

flashcards.q: What is the standard form of a quadratic equation?
flashcards.a: ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0.

flashcards.q: What is the formula for the discriminant?
flashcards.a: Δ = b² − 4ac.

flashcards.q: What is the nature of roots when Δ < 0?
flashcards.a: The roots are not real.

flashcards.q: What is the sum of the roots of ax² + bx + c = 0?
flashcards.a: α + β = −b/a.

flashcards.q: State the quadratic formula.

practice.q: Solve the quadratic equation x² − 15x + 54 = 0 by factorization.
practice.a:
x² − 15x + 54 = 0.
Two numbers whose product is 54 and sum is −15 are −6 and −9.
So (x−6)(x−9) = 0.
Answer: x = 6 or x = 9.

practice.q: Form a quadratic equation whose roots are 3 and −7.
practice.a:
Sum of roots = 3+(−7) = −4;
Product of roots = 3×(−7) = −21.
Equation: x² − (sum)x + (product) = 0.
Answer: x² + 4x − 21 = 0.

practice.q: Determine the nature of the roots of the quadratic equation 3x² − 5x + 7 = 0.
practice.a:
Here a=3, b=−5, c=7. Δ = b²−4ac = (−5)² − 4(3)(7) = 25 − 84 = −59.
Since Δ < 0, the roots are not real (imaginary).

practice.q: Two numbers differ by 3, and their product is 504. Form a quadratic equation and find the numbers.
practice.a:
Let the numbers be x and x+3.
Given x(x+3) = 504
⟹ x² + 3x − 504 = 0. Δ = 9 + 2016 = 2025 = 45². x = (−3±45)/2
⟹ x = 21 or x = −24.
Answer: The two numbers are 21 and 24 (or −24 and −21, the negative pair also satisfies the condition).

practice.q: The sum of a number and its reciprocal is 25/12. Find the number by forming a quadratic equation and solving it using the formula.
practice.a:
Let the number be x. x + 1/x = 25/12.
Multiplying throughout by 12x: 12x² + 12 = 25x
⟹ 12x² − 25x + 12 = 0. Δ = 625 − 576 = 49 = 7². x = (25±7)/24
⟹ x = 32/24 = 4/3 or x = 18/24 = 3/4.
Answer: The number is 4/3 or 3/4.

chapters.title: Arithmetic Progression
notes: Sequence: An arrangement of numbers following a definite pattern is called a sequence. Each number in a sequence is called a term.

Arithmetic Progression (A.P.): A sequence in which the difference between any term and its preceding term is constant is called an Arithmetic Progression. This constant difference is called the common difference, denoted by d.
Common Difference: The common difference is calculated as d = tn+1 − tn, where tn is the nth term. If d is positive the A.P. is increasing, and if d is negative the A.P. is decreasing.

General (nth) Term of an A.P.: The nth term of an A.P. with first term a and common difference d is given by tn = a + (n − 1)d. This formula allows finding any term without listing all preceding terms.

Sum of First n Terms: The sum of the first n terms of an A.P. is Sn = n/2 [2a + (n − 1)d], which can also be written as Sn = n/2 (a + l), where l is the last term. This formula is essential for solving problems involving totals.
Relation between tn and Sn: The nth term of an A.P. can also be found using tn = Sn − Sn−1. This relationship connects the sum formula with the general term formula.

Arithmetic Mean: When three numbers are in A.P., the middle term is called the arithmetic mean of the other two, given by A.M. = (a + b)/2. This concept is used to insert terms between two given numbers.
